_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Confirmed diagnosis of Choledochal cyst (excluding Type III where endoscopic sphincterotomy is adequate) by imaging (Ultrasound Abdomen, MRCP, ERCP/CT Abdomen).
Presence of clinical symptoms such as vague right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, nausea, vomiting, abdominal distension, recurrent attacks with abdominal pain and pyrexia.
High risk of malignancy and associated complications (ductal strictures, stone formation, cholangitis, rupture, and secondary biliary cirrhosis).

preauth.label: Clinical notes including evaluation findings, indication for procedure, and planned line of management

claim.label: Detailed Indoor case papers (ICPs) with treatment details

ppd_preauth.q: Are clinical notes with detailed history, signs & symptoms, planned treatment line, and indication for procedure available?
Did imaging (USG/MRCP/ERCP/CT Abdomen) confirm the diagnosis?
Are the clinical details and imaging indicative of surgery?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details available?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Was USG/CT/MRCP/ERCP Abdomen report indicative of surgery?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advice at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Clinical notes with detailed history, signs & symptoms, planned treatment line, or indication for procedure are missing or incomplete at pre-authorization.
Imaging (USG/MRCP/ERCP/CT Abdomen) did not confirm the diagnosis of choledochal cyst at pre-authorization.
Clinical details and/or imaging were not indicative of surgery for choledochal cyst.
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) with daily vitals and treatment details are missing or incomplete at claim submission.
Detailed Procedure / Operative Notes are missing or incomplete at claim submission.
Discharge summary with follow-up advice is missing or incomplete at claim submission.
Mandatory imaging reports (Ultrasound Abdomen, MRCP) are missing at pre-authorization.

clinical_key_pointers: Choledochal cyst (biliary cysts) is defined as an isolated or combined congenital dilatation of the extra or intrahepatic biliary tree.
Choledochal cyst is not an isolated entity but rather regarded as a constellation of pathological anomalies in the hepato-pancreaticobiliary system.
Biliary cysts are associated with significant complications such as ductal strictures, stone formation, cholangitis, rupture, and secondary biliary cirrhosis.
In addition, certain types of biliary cysts have a high risk of malignancy.
Majority of cases manifest in children within 1-2 years of age. It can also present in adults.
Abdominal distension can be due to a large cyst. The cyst can be palpated per abdomen in the right hypochondrium.
Slow progressive jaundice, recurrent attacks with abdominal pain and pyrexia.
Patients may also report nausea, vomiting, fever, pruritus, and weight loss.

Infants typically present with obstructive jaundice and abdominal masses, children more often present with abdominal pain and nausea/vomiting, and adults frequently present with pain, fever, nausea/vomiting, and jaundice.
surgical_options: Cyst excision with Roux-en-Y hepatoenterostomy is the definitive treatment of choice in all patients with Choledochal cyst, regardless of age or symptomatology.
Type I: Excision of the cyst followed by Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy or hepaticoduodenostomy.
Type II: Excision of the diverticulum with suturing of CBD.

Type IV: Complete excision with Roux-en-Y Hepaticojejunostomy; If total excision is not possible due to adhesions between the cyst and portal vein, posterior wall of the cyst can be left behind, after removal of mucosa (Lilly's technique); Hepatectomy for excision of the cyst, and reconstruction with wide hilar Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy (for Type IV A/V).
